full_response:
Step 1 :  Set up the three frames  
• King-Arthur’s rest frame 𝒦 : coordinates (t, x).  
• Knight #1’s rest frame 𝑲₁ : not needed in this part of the problem.  
• Knight #2’s rest frame 𝑲₂ : coordinates (t₂, x₂).  
Knight #2 moves with speed v (β = v/c) in the +x-direction with respect to 𝒦.  
The Lorentz–boost from 𝒦 to 𝑲₂ is therefore

 ct₂ = γ (ct − β x)  
 x₂  = γ (x − β ct)                     (1)

with γ = 1/√(1−β²).

Step 2 :  Fix the origins of the two frames  
By convention we make the origins of 𝑲 and 𝑲₂ coincide when Knight #2 is at his own starting line.  
That event (Knight #2 at his start) we call E₂ and locate at

 In 𝒦 : E₂ → (t=0 , x = L)  
 In 𝑲₂ : E₂ → (t₂=0 , x₂=0) .

Thus any other event with coordinates (t, x) in 𝒦 must first be written as the displacement

 Δt = t − 0 = t,  Δx = x − L                     (2)

measured from E₂, before applying the boost (1).

Step 3 :  Coordinates of event E₁ in 𝒦  
Event E₁ is “Knight #1 crosses his own starting line”.  
According to King Arthur,

 E₁ → (t=0 , x = 0).                               (3)

Step 4 :  Displacement of E₁ from E₂ in 𝒦  
From (2) and (3)

 Δt = 0 , Δx = −L.                                 (4)

Step 5 :  Transform to Knight #2’s frame  
Insert (4) in the Lorentz formulas (1) (replace t→Δt, x→Δx):

 ct₂ = γ ( c·0 − β(−L) ) = γ β L ,  
 x₂  = γ ( −L − β·0 )   = −γ L.                    (5)

Step 6 :  Solve for t₂  
Divide the first of (5) by c:

 t₂ = γ β L / c .                                  (6)

Result  
Event E₁ occurs in Knight #2’s frame at

 t₂ = γ β L / c,  x₂ = −γ L.
